name: Sliding Block
description: Players move blocks across constrained spaces to cover targets, open routes, or reach a solved arrangement.
design_notes: Sliding block puzzles need grid coordinates, move locks, collision checks, reset anchors, undo depth, and solvability validation. Level authors should be able to prove the starting layout can reach the solution without relying on perfect physics behavior.
design_purpose: Create spatial reasoning challenges around occupancy, routes, and irreversible-looking moves.
Let players transform room layout by moving large objects into useful positions.
Support pressure plates, bridges, switches, and path-blocking puzzles with visible state.
player_fantasy: The player feels clever when they arrange heavy blocks into the one layout that makes the room work.

parameters.name: block_instance_id
parameters.description: Stable block identifier used for movement, saves, undo, and reset anchors.

parameters.name: grid_cell_coordinate
parameters.description: Current authored grid cell occupied by the block.

parameters.name: move_lock_state
parameters.description: State that prevents overlapping pushes or interaction during movement.

parameters.name: reset_anchor_id
parameters.description: Anchor used when the puzzle resets a block after failure or player request.

parameters.name: undo_stack_depth
parameters.description: Number of recent block moves available for player undo or debug replay.

parameters.name: solvability_check_id
parameters.description: Validation pass used by tools or runtime to detect soft-locked layouts.

edge_cases: A block is pushed into a corner where no legal move can recover it.
The player saves after a block starts moving but before it reaches the next grid cell.
Two blocks are pushed into the same destination cell by simultaneous interactions.
A physics body or enemy occupies a cell that the grid solver considers empty.
common_bugs: Block visuals move smoothly but grid_cell_coordinate updates early, allowing invalid overlapping moves.
Undo restores position but not linked pressure plate or door state.
Reset returns blocks to anchors while the player is standing in the destination cell.
Solvability checks ignore one-way movement rules and approve impossible layouts.
balancing_notes: Small block puzzles benefit from undo; larger puzzles need reset shortcuts and readable goals.
Irreversible moves can create tension but should be used sparingly unless reset is fast.
Block weight, push cadence, and camera framing should support reasoning rather than slow the player down.
accessibility_notes: Provide undo or reset options for long spatial puzzles to reduce repetitive recovery.
Use high-contrast target markers and grid alignment cues so block goals are readable.

implementation_notes.unity: Keep authoritative grid occupancy separate from rendered block transforms. A push request should reserve destination cell, set move_lock_state, animate toward the cell, then commit linked puzzle events after arrival.
implementation_notes.godot: Use TileMap or custom grid data for occupancy, with block nodes reading block_instance_id and grid_cell_coordinate from puzzle state. Store undo records containing block id, previous cell, next cell, and linked target deltas.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use an Actor Component for block grid state and movement lock, with traces validating destination cells. Server or single-player authority should commit cell changes and multicast animation from the accepted move result.
implementation_notes.web: Represent the puzzle as a grid model first and render blocks from model cells. Phaser tweens or canvas interpolation should never be the source of truth for occupancy or undo.

example_games.title: Sokoban
example_games.note: Uses block pushing and grid occupancy as the core puzzle structure.

example_games.title: The Legend of Zelda: Link's Awakening
example_games.note: Uses pushable block arrangements in dungeon rooms.
